one_line_takeaway: Simply prompting a capable off-the-shelf LLM to detect and excise injected instructions drives prompt-injection attack success on AgentDojo from ~55% to near 0% with sub-1% false-positive/negative rates and no utility loss, but its strength scales with the guardrail model's capability.
key_finding: On AgentDojo, PromptArmor with strong guardrail LLMs nearly eliminates the attack while preserving (even improving) utility. PromptArmor-GPT-4.1 reaches 0.00% ASR (vs. 54.53% undefended) with 0.56% FPR, 0.13% FNR, and 72.02% UA (up from 64.27% undefended); PromptArmor-o4-mini gives the highest utility at 76.35% UA with 0.08% ASR. Even GPT-3.5, released before AgentDojo, provides substantial protection (6.84% ASR), and its detection depends on being given a definition of 'prompt injection' (FNR drops from 60.24% to 15.74%, ASR from 34.50% to 6.84% once the definition is added). Model capacity is the dominant factor: Qwen3-32B ~ matches GPT-4.1, while Qwen3-0.6B shows extreme trade-offs (62.57% FPR without reasoning, 75.71% FNR with reasoning). The memorization test yields average similarity 0.34 with only 3.5% of samples above the 0.6 threshold, arguing performance is not memorization. PromptArmor stays robust under the AgentVigil adaptive attack (0.34% ASR adaptively vs. 36.99% undefended). It also outperforms all six baselines, several of which either fail to defend (Delimiter 51.51% ASR, DataSentinel 38.63% ASR) or sacrifice utility (Tool Filter 0.79% ASR but only 18.80% UA).
core_contribution: PromptArmor, a detection-and-removal defense against prompt injection attacks on LLM agents. Rather than fine-tuning a detector or the backend model, it prompts an off-the-shelf 'guardrail' LLM (which may be the same model as the agent's backend) with a carefully designed prompt to (1) decide Yes/No whether a data sample contains an injected prompt and (2) if Yes, output the injected span. The reported span is then stripped from the data via a fuzzy-matching regex (allowing arbitrary characters between the extracted words to tolerate whitespace/punctuation drift), and the sanitized data is passed to the agent so the original user task can still complete. The paper's own novelty claim is the prompting strategy plus the empirical demonstration that modern off-the-shelf LLMs CAN be prompted to defend effectively, challenging the prior belief (attributed to Liu et al. 2024/2025) that they cannot. Note: instruction-hierarchy, StruQ, SecAlign, DataSentinel, Deberta, MELON, CaMeL, IsolateGPT, Progent, spotlighting, etc. are cited related/baseline work, NOT this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Indirect prompt injection against tool-using LLM agents: an attacker embeds malicious instructions into an untrusted external data source (e.g., an email, web page, or transaction record) that the agent retrieves via a tool call. When the contaminated tool output is fed back into the agent's context, the injected instruction hijacks the agent's execution flow so it performs an attacker-specified task (e.g., exfiltrating data, sending money to the attacker) instead of the intended user task. The paper explicitly scopes this apart from jailbreaking (which targets an LLM's safety alignment). Attacks are assumed black-box (the paper deliberately excludes white-box attacks like GCG and attention-tracking, arguing deployed agents use black-box models). The authors note the guardrail LLM being itself injectable (e.g., GPT-4.1 as a backend suffers 54.53% ASR undefended) does not prevent it from serving as an effective detector.
limitations: Evaluated on a single benchmark (AgentDojo) with one backend model (GPT-4.1); generalization to other agent frameworks/backends is asserted rather than measured. Effectiveness hinges on guardrail-LLM capability: weaker/smaller models degrade sharply (GPT-3.5: 11.24% FPR / 15.74% FNR; Qwen3-0.6B unusable), and GPT-3.5 needed a hand-supplied definition of the term, indicating brittleness to prompt wording. The defense adds a full extra LLM inference call per data sample (the paper frames this as acceptable but does not report latency/cost). Removal relies on fuzzy-matching the guardrail's extracted span, which can misfire if the model's extraction is imprecise. The threat scope is limited to black-box attacks: white-box attacks (GCG, attention tracking) and training-based defense comparisons (SecAlign, excluded for poor AgentDojo utility) are out of scope. The authors concede the guardrail LLM is itself vulnerable to injection, so a sufficiently strong second-order injection targeting the guardrail's own detection prompt remains a residual risk beyond the one AgentVigil adaptive setting tested.

datasets_used: AgentDojo benchmark (Debenedetti et al., 2024): banking/slack/travel/workspace environments with 16/21/20/40 user tasks, 629 total adversarial user-task x injection-task scenarios
AgentDojo's four built-in attacks: Ignore Previous Instructions, System Message, Important Messages, Tool Knowledge
AgentVigil (Wang et al., 2025c) fuzzing-generated adaptive attack templates (top-5 by ASR selected)
quantitative_results: No defense: 64.27% UA, 54.53% combined ASR (FPR/FNR N/A)
PromptArmor-GPT-3.5: 11.24% FPR, 15.74% FNR, 51.35% UA, 6.84% ASR (worst config)
Prompting ablation, GPT-3.5 without definition: 0.06% FPR, 60.24% FNR, 70.07% UA, 34.50% ASR; with definition: 11.24% FPR, 15.74% FNR, 51.35% UA, 6.84% ASR
Qwen3-0.6B: 62.57% FPR (non-reasoning), 75.71% FNR (reasoning) — extreme trade-off; Qwen3-32B: FPR/FNR ~0.99-1.14% approaching GPT-4.1
Memorization test on GPT-4.1: average edit-distance similarity 0.34, only 3.5% of samples above 0.6 threshold

Adaptive attack (GPT-4.1 guardrail): AgentVigil-NoDefense undefended 70.48% ASR -> PromptArmor 0.00% ASR (0.63% FPR, 4.86% FNR); AgentVigil-Adaptive undefended 36.99% ASR -> PromptArmor 0.34% ASR (0.70% FPR, 2.26% FNR)
baselines_compared: Deberta (ProtectAI detection model): 28.41% FPR, 22.03% FNR, 29.73% UA, 18.92% ASR
DataSentinel (Liu et al. 2025, detection): 0.10% FPR, 48.78% FNR, 46.38% UA, 38.63% ASR
Tool Filter (Debenedetti et al. 2024): 18.80% UA, 0.79% ASR (low ASR at severe utility cost)
MELON (Zhu et al. 2025, system-level): 58.62% UA, 3.18% ASR
